does: This paper conducts a rapid systematic evidence scan of 13 AI risk mitigation frameworks published between 2023-2025, extracting 831 documented mitigations into a living database. The authors iteratively cluster and code these mitigations to construct a preliminary taxonomy that organizes AI risk mitigations into four primary categories—Governance & Oversight, Technical & Security, Operational Process, and Information & Ecosystem—further subdivided into 23 subcategories. The taxonomy aims to create a unified conceptual framework with consistent terminology that consolidates fragmented landscape of AI risk management approaches across organizations and governments developing, deploying, and governing AI systems.
connects: This work responds to the fragmentation and inconsistency plaguing contemporary AI governance and risk management, building on prior efforts to systematize safety approaches in machine learning. It extends earlier taxonomic work in AI safety and governance by bringing together multiple frameworks developed during the period of rapid advancement in large language models and foundation models. The paper engages with established lines of research on AI oversight mechanisms, technical safety measures, and governance structures from organizations like NIST, the EU, and academic institutions, while synthesizing newer frameworks that address emerging risks from advanced AI systems.
enables: This taxonomy provides a shared vocabulary and organizational structure that facilitates coordination among AI developers, policymakers, and oversight bodies working on risk mitigation. By identifying coverage gaps and overlaps across existing frameworks, the work enables more systematic assessment of mitigation strategies and can guide future policy development and technical research priorities. The living database structure supports iterative refinement as new mitigations emerge, potentially serving as an infrastructure for tracking the evolution of AI risk management practices and identifying which approaches are most widely adopted or particularly effective.
